title: ICMR Hypertension Management Guidelines
content: Target BP for most adults: <130/80 mmHg. For patients >65 years: <140/90 mmHg. First-line therapy: ACE inhibitors or ARBs for diabetic patients with hypertension. Thiazide diuretics for isolated systolic hypertension in elderly. Lifestyle modifications: sodium restriction <5g/day, regular aerobic exercise 30 min/day, weight reduction.

title: Hypertension Red Flags
content: Hypertensive urgency: BP >180/120 mmHg without end-organ damage. Hypertensive emergency: BP >180/120 mmHg WITH end-organ damage (chest pain, dyspnoea, neurological deficit, visual changes, acute kidney injury). Requires immediate IV therapy and ICU admission. Do not lower BP rapidly — risk of watershed infarction.

title: ICMR Diabetes Management Guidelines
content: HbA1c target: <7% for most adults, <8% for elderly/frail. First-line: Metformin 500mg BD with meals (if eGFR >30). Add SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) if cardiovascular disease present. Add GLP-1 agonist if obesity. Monitor renal function annually. Screen for diabetic retinopathy annually. Foot examination every visit.

title: Hypoglycaemia Management
content: Hypoglycaemia: blood glucose <70 mg/dL with symptoms OR <54 mg/dL regardless of symptoms. Treat conscious patient: 15g fast-acting carbohydrate (3-4 glucose tablets or 150mL fruit juice). Recheck in 15 minutes. Unconscious patient: IV glucose 25mL of 50% dextrose. Educate on sick-day rules. Review insulin dose.

title: Acute Febrile Illness - Primary Care Protocol
content: For febrile illness in India: Always screen for malaria (peripheral smear or RDT) if fever >3 days. Dengue NS1 antigen in first 5 days, IgM/IgG from day 5. Typhoid Widal test (unreliable — use culture if available). Blood culture if fever >7 days or severe illness. Treat empirically for malaria in high-transmission areas without waiting for results.

title: Sepsis Recognition and Management
content: Sepsis: life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. qSOFA score ≥2 (altered mental status, RR ≥22, SBP ≤100) suggests sepsis. Action within 1 hour: blood cultures x2, IV antibiotics, IV fluids 30mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive, check lactate. Septic shock: vasopressors needed. Mortality increases 7% per hour without antibiotics.

title: Community Acquired Pneumonia Management
content: CAP severity: CURB-65 score (Confusion, Urea >7, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, age ≥65). Score 0-1: outpatient. Score 2: hospital. Score 3+: ICU consider. Antibiotic: Amoxicillin 500mg TDS for mild CAP. Add azithromycin if atypicals suspected. Chest X-ray to confirm. Follow-up in 6 weeks to confirm resolution. Oxygen if SpO2 <94%.

title: Paediatric Fever Management
content: Fever in children <3 months: all require immediate evaluation and likely hospitalisation. Danger signs: lethargy, poor feeding, bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, petechial rash (meningococcal), stridor, severe respiratory distress, cyanosis. Refer immediately. For fever alone in child >3 months without danger signs: antipyretics (paracetamol 15mg/kg), oral fluids, return if not improving in 48-72 hours.

title: Antibiotic Stewardship - Primary Care
content: Most URTIs (common cold, pharyngitis, sinusitis) are viral — antibiotics not indicated. Only prescribe antibiotics if: bacterial pneumonia, streptococcal pharyngitis (exudates + fever + LAP), UTI (symptoms + dipstick), skin and soft tissue infection. Avoid broad-spectrum antibiotics as first line. Document indication, dose, duration. Review at 48-72 hours.

title: Mental Health Screening in Primary Care
content: Depression screening: PHQ-9. Score ≥10 suggests moderate depression. Anxiety: GAD-7. Score ≥10 suggests moderate anxiety. Always ask about suicidal ideation if PHQ-9 question 9 positive. Refer to psychiatry if: psychosis, suicidal risk, severe depression. Primary care can initiate SSRI (sertraline 50mg OD) for moderate depression with 4-week follow-up. Counsel on sleep hygiene, exercise, social support.
